section_title: Post-Hoc Retrodiction — prediction vs retrodiction distinction

fingerprint_1.name: The 18.6-year lunar nodal regression period is itself a spherical-geometry quantity
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: Section 2b.3 correctly identifies WIN-020 as retrodictive: gear ratios are 'a mechanism for matching a pre-known value, not a derivation from dome parameters.' The section nails the logical structure. That critique stands.
fingerprint_1.deeper_analysis: The section leaves the 18.6 years hanging as if it were a neutral number the dome happens to match. It is not neutral. The 18.6 years is derivable ONLY from spherical-Earth celestial mechanics: inverse-square gravity between Earth and Moon, plus solar tidal perturbation on the Moon’s inclined Keplerian orbit around a spherical Earth. Laplace derived it analytically in 1805; Brown’s lunar theory gives it to parts per million; Babylonian astronomers observed it via the Saros cycle. No flat-disc gravitational scheme produces this period. The gears retrodicting a specifically globe output by construction.
fingerprint_1.globe_derivation: 18.6 y = 2pi / Omega_node, where Omega_node is the nodal regression rate from solar perturbation on the lunar orbit. Every input — Keplerian mean motion, semimajor axis (384400 km), lunar inclination (5.145 deg) — presupposes spherical orbital geometry. The dome publishes no derivation of 18.6 y from DISC_R, LUNAR_H, or any other dome parameter.
fingerprint_1.code_search_result: GitHub code search returned 0 hits for 18.6, nodal, gear, epicyclic, saros, lunar_node in john09289/predictions. WIN-020 exists only on the website, not in any computational script.
fingerprint_1.suggested_text: Append to WIN-020 paragraph: 'The 18.6-year period itself is a specifically spherical-Earth quantity, derivable from the Moon’s inclined Keplerian orbit around a spherical Earth under solar perturbation (Laplace 1805; Brown 1896). No flat-disc gravitational scheme produces this period. The gears therefore reproduce a globe quantity with free parameters — not a dome-native prediction but a globe prediction retrofitted into dome-shaped machinery.'

fingerprint_2.name: The dome-derived count collapses: all four geometric WINs use dome parameters reverse-engineered from WGS84 via find_curve.py
fingerprint_2.kernel_of_truth: Section 2b.3 already notes WIN-058 is 'reverse-engineered from WGS84 route distances via least-squares fitting' and WIN-057 compares against WGS84. But it still counts the dome-derived subset as nonzero.
fingerprint_2.deeper_analysis: Tracing the derivation chain shows the effective count of globe-free derivations is zero. The script find_curve.py in the dome repository uses explicit WGS84 great-circle distances (London-New York 5570 km, Cape Town-Buenos Aires 6860 km, etc.) and the globe-latitude formula r = (90 - lat) * 111.1 (the AE projection identity) to solve for dome parameters. DISC_R, H0, and lambda_G are all outputs of these fits. Any prediction downstream of these constants inherits globe geometry through the fit chain.

key_lines: real_dist dict: WGS84 city-pair distances for 8 globe cities
Comment: 'Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius'
Solves for H at each latitude so r*tan(lat) matches H(r)*exp pattern

chain_for_each_WIN.WIN_044: FSF uses H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619). Both 8537 and 8619 are find_curve.py outputs against WGS84.
chain_for_each_WIN.WIN_057: Two-zone radii fitted to WGS84. Section already notes accuracy vs WGS84 baseline.
chain_for_each_WIN.WIN_020: LUNAR_H = 24.84 h in monitor.py is globe sidereal lunar period. Gears built from globe periods reproducing a globe period.

fingerprint_2.suggested_text: Append to 'The Derives-from-Dome Problem': 'The dome constants in these derivations (DISC_R = 20015 km, H0 = 8537 km, lambda = 8619 km) are themselves outputs of fit routines in the repository (find_curve.py, test_curve_stretching.py) that solve against WGS84 great-circle distances and globe latitudes. find_curve.py uses the azimuthal-equidistant formula r = (90 - lat) * 111.1 km and a dictionary of WGS84 city-pair distances as ground truth. Parameters fitted this way cannot produce predictions independent of the globe. The effective number of genuinely globe-independent dome derivations is zero.'

honorable_mentions.prospective_WINs: Threshold checks on NOAA/INTERMAGNET trends computed in WGS84 frame. Already correctly called out in the section.
honorable_mentions.eclipse_E_PRED: Eclipse timing from JPL ephemerides (spherical mechanics). Section fairly frames this as 'best opportunity for genuine prospective test.'
honorable_mentions.LUNAR_H_constant: monitor.py LUNAR_H = 24.84 approximates globe sidereal lunar period 24.8412 h. Part of WIN-020 inheritance chain.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_1: Fitting to WGS84 is just coordinate transformation, not a globe fingerprint.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_1: A coordinate transformation preserves geometry. Here the fit produces SPECIFIC numerical values that would differ if you fit to a different geometry. The dome's numerical agreement is a property of which fit target was used, not of dome physics. Choosing the target first, fitting parameters, then claiming the match is retrodiction by definition.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: 18.6 years is empirical. The dome gears reproduce it regardless of how the globe derives it.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: Reproducing an observation with free parameters is curve-fitting, not prediction. Globe derives 18.6 y from ZERO free parameters (Keplerian mechanics). Dome uses multiple free gears chosen to match. A zero-free-parameter match is informative; an adjustable-parameter match cannot fail and is not.

newsworthy: Dome find_curve.py fits every dome parameter against WGS84 globe distances — so even the small subset of 'dome derivations' inherits the globe through its constants
